articles.title: The Scapegoat
articles.titleZh: 替罪羊？McClatchy新闻社证明：真正驱动变革的是人类而非AI
articles.summary: 尽管媒体大肆渲染AI对新闻业的冲击，McClatchy的案例表明，真正推动行业变化的仍是人类记者和编辑的决策。AI只是工具，最终价值仍取决于人的判断与创造力。
articles.summaryEn: Despite media hype about AI disrupting journalism, McClatchy's experience shows humans remain the primary drivers of change. AI serves as a tool, but ultimate value depends on human judgment and creative decisions.
articles.reason: 提醒我们警惕技术决定论陷阱，回归人对技术的掌控本质。

fullText.siteName: Tedium: The Dull Side of the Internet.

blocks.text: McClatchy is a company that screams legacy. Nearly 170 years old, it has acquired a number of significant newspapers over the years, most notably in 2006, when it acquired the iconic Knight Ridder chain.

It is a company that has faced many challenges over its long history, notably filing for bankruptcy around the time of the COVID-19 outbreak. Even after merging with the former owner of the National Enquirer (really), it is barely holding on, and plus it has to figure out this whole AI thing.

One of my favorite metaphors is the idea of using a wrench in place of a hammer. It technically works, but it’s not the right purpose. AI tools are often the wrench of technology. And McClatchy just found its wrench.

According to The Wrap (paywall), the chain is pushing its journalists to use AI tech to repackage content in multiple directions. The technology was sold to the employees as Grammarly on steroids, and the hint seems to be that those who don’t accept this technology will be on thin ice career-wise.

“Journalists who embrace and experiment with this tool are going to win,” McClatchy VP of Local News Eric Nelson said recently, per the publication. “Journalists who are defiant will fall behind. Bottom line: We need more stories and we need more inventory.”

McClatchy is effectively using Claude to take already-written stories, repackage the reporting, and reuse it in whatever ways are necessary. Put another way, the company is trying to scale up for the arms race that is SEO, social media, and Google Discover.

The problem is, that means that these journalists are now going to have their bylines on content that AI actively wrote and repackaged, while attempting to limit the say those journalists have in the matter. From the piece:

Kathy Vetter, McClatchy’s chief of staff for local news, said during the March 17 meeting that the company’s general policy was that reporters who cannot revoke the use of their bylines must keep them attached to CSA-produced stories. For those who can revoke their byline, she said, McClatchy will still use their work anyway.“We have every right to use their work,” she said, according to multiple sources familiar with the meeting. “It belongs to us, and if an editor wants to go … in there and repurpose a reporter’s content, they can put their name on it.”
Unions have gotten involved, limiting how those bylines get used, but not every paper has a union.
An unwanted byline introduces murky questions

What’s fascinating about the Wrap piece is the divide between journalists and executives that it exposes. VPs and business staffers seem excited about the opportunities this opens up. Journalists are upset that their names are going to be associated with work they didn’t actually write.

I’m not a lawyer, but the decision to essentially force non-unionized employees to include their bylines on pieces they didn’t write feels like it could be legally risky to me. Let me pose a scenario: Let’s say one of these LLM stories gets something wrong, and a journalist gets strong pushback on social media about the story, maybe even death threats, even though they didn’t write it. Does that put the newspaper at risk of a lawsuit from their own employee? Given our current culture, that does not seem far-fetched.

There are other risks, too: Imagine a defamation lawsuit against a journalist based on an error AI introduced, for example. And for readers, it might introduce a misrepresentation risk that gets a regulator like the Federal Trade Commission to weigh in, potentially even restricting the use of AI in news content. The parallels to the Wild West of early adtech are hard to miss.

If it was the government forcing this situation, that byline might even be seen as “compelled speech,” though employers have a lot more leverage. Nonetheless, it points at a moral wrong of sorts, a breaking of norms, and one that feels avoidable. After all, journalists typically have the right to take their bylines off of pieces, even if McClatchy appears to be quietly eliminating that right.
By McClatchy attempting to make this shift, it highlights the weakening state of the power dynamic between the newsroom and its employees. And AI is the justification.
A truism about AI: It’s often a scapegoat
Another headline that I stumbled upon around the same time I think points to a broader issue: Often, AI is just used as a reason to do something that employees would otherwise be uncomfortable with.

This week, Meta announced a plan to start tracking employees’ mouse and keyboard input, with the idea of building training data for its AI agents. See, it’s okay if we spy on you, because it’s for AI.
Let’s be clear, if Meta wanted to do this, it would just do it. It doesn’t need to attach AI as an excuse. But the addition makes it generally more palatable.

Likewise, if McClatchy wanted to have a bunch of inexperienced interns or non-journalists repackage content in haphazard, over-the-top ways, it could just do it. If it wanted to strip employees of the right to take their name off a story, it could just do it. But AI gives it enough of a sheen that it takes attention off the fact there’s nothing stopping them from just doing it because today is a day that ends in y.

And I think that’s ultimately the point I want to get at here. Employers are going to say a lot of things in the coming years and blame AI for doing those things. After all, it’s a great wrench for hammering in nails. But let’s not be silly: It’s also an excellent excuse to sweep a lot of other changes through, whether it’s layoffs or costing employees some of their taken-for-granted rights.
In Wizard of Oz parlance, don’t let the flashy visuals fool you: There’s a human behind the curtain, making the choices that could reshape your life and career.
Wrench-Free Links

So John Ternus is gonna be Apple’s new CEO. Good for him, it’s a well-deserved promotion and it could help make Apple a little less conservative with some of its decision-making. One thing hinted about in recent coverage was that the MacBook Neo was his baby, and its success proved to Tim Cook that he was leaving Apple in good hands. Sounds like a good first sign.

The new Beck single, “Ride Lonesome,” is such a weird tune. It sounds like he intentionally went back to “The Golden Age,” the leadoff track of his classic breakup album Sea Change, changed a chord or two, and shipped it off to the label. He’s lucky that his music is so good that he can John Fogerty himself.
Shout-out to the new pasta sauce microphone manufacturer, Prego.
Find this one an interesting read? Share it with a pal! And back at it soon.
And thanks again to Setapp for sponsoring.

translations: McClatchy 是一家典型的传统媒体公司。这家拥有近170年历史的企业，多年来收购了不少重要报纸，其中最著名的便是2006年收购的标志性 Knight Ridder 报业连锁。
在其漫长的历史中，McClatchy 面临过诸多挑战，最引人注目的是在新冠疫情爆发期间申请破产保护。即便后来与《国家询问报》（National Enquirer）的前所有者合并，公司依然岌岌可危，还得想办法应对人工智能（AI）这一新事物。
我最喜欢的一个比喻是：用扳手当锤子使。虽然技术上可行，但显然不是正确用途。AI 工具往往就像是技术领域的“扳手”，而 McClatchy 最近刚拿到了一把。
据 The Wrap 报道（需订阅），该报业集团正要求旗下记者使用 AI 技术，将已有内容以多种方式重新包装发布。这项技术被宣传为“Grammarly 的升级版”，而且似乎暗示不接受这项技术的记者在职场上可能会处境艰难。
“拥抱并尝试使用这款工具的记者将会胜出，”McClatchy 地方新闻副总裁 Eric Nelson 近日对媒体表示。“那些抗拒的记者则会落后。”简而言之：我们需要更多故事，也需要更多内容库存。
实际上，McClatchy 正在利用 Claude 模型，将已经写好的报道重新包装，并以各种必要形式重复使用。换句话说，该公司正试图在 SEO、社交媒体和 Google Discover 的激烈竞争中扩大规模。
问题在于，这意味着这些记者的名字将被署在他们并未实际撰写的、由 AI 主动创作并重新包装的内容上，同时还要限制他们对这些内容的发言权。正如文章所述：
地方新闻主管 Kathy Vetter 在3月17日的会议上表示，公司的一般政策是：如果记者无法撤回其署名权，就必须将其名字保留在 CSA 制作的故事中。对于能够撤回署名的记者，她表示 McClatchy 仍会使用他们的作品。“我们完全有权使用他们的作品，”她说道，多位了解会议情况的消息人士证实。“作品属于公司，如果编辑想要……在那里重新利用记者的内容，他们可以把自己的名字加上去。”
工会已介入此事，限制了这些署名的使用方式，但并非所有报社都有工会组织。
非自愿署名带来了模糊不清的问题。
《The Wrap》的报道最令人着迷之处在于，它揭示了记者与管理层之间的分歧。副总裁和业务部门人员似乎对这项技术带来的机遇感到兴奋；而记者们则对名字将与自己未参与创作的作品联系在一起感到不满。
我不是律师，但强制要求非工会员工在自己未撰写的 LLM 生成内容上署名，这种做法在我看来可能存在法律风险。让我设想一个场景：假设其中一篇由 LLM 生成的文章出现错误，一位记者因此遭到社交媒体上的强烈抵制，甚至可能收到死亡威胁——尽管他并未撰写该文章。这是否会让报社面临来自自身员工的诉讼风险？考虑到当前的社会环境，这似乎并非空穴来风。
还有其他风险：比如，想象一下，一篇由 AI 引入错误而引发的针对记者的诽谤诉讼。对读者而言，这可能带来误导性陈述的风险，从而引发联邦贸易委员会等监管机构介入，甚至可能限制 AI 在新闻内容中的应用。这种与早期广告技术“狂野西部”时期的相似之处显而易见。
如果是政府强制推行这一局面，这种署名行为甚至可能被视作“被迫言论”，尽管雇主拥有更大的话语权。然而，这仍反映出某种道德上的失范，一种可避免的规范破坏。毕竟，记者通常有权从文章中撤下自己的署名，即便 McClatchy 似乎正悄然剥夺这一权利。
通过 McClatchy 试图推动这一转变，凸显了新闻编辑部与其员工之间权力关系的弱化。而 AI 正是其正当化理由。
关于 AI 的一个普遍观点是：它常常成为替罪羊。
另一则我在同一时期偶然看到的标题，指向了一个更广泛的问题：AI 常被用作员工原本不愿实施某事的借口。
本周，Meta 宣布了一项计划，将开始追踪员工的鼠标和键盘输入，目的是为其 AI 代理构建训练数据。你看，如果我们监视你，那是因为这是为了 AI。
让我们明确一点：如果 Meta 真想这么做，它早就这么做了。它无需借助 AI 作为借口。但加上这一理由，整体上更容易被接受。
同样地，如果 McClatchy 想安排一群缺乏经验的新手实习生或非记者随意、夸张地重编内容，它可以这么做；如果想剥夺员工从报道中移除署名的权利，它也可以这么做。但 AI 赋予了其行动一层光环，让人忽略了他们本就可以直接这么做——毕竟今天是个以 y 结尾的日子。
我认为这才是本文真正想传达的核心观点：未来几年，雇主们会找各种理由，把责任推给 AI。毕竟，AI 是一把很好的锤子，用来钉钉子。但我们别自欺欺人了：它同样是一个绝佳的借口，可以顺带推动许多其他变革，无论是裁员，还是剥夺员工那些理所当然的权利。
用《绿野仙踪》的话说：别被华丽的外表迷惑，幕后其实站着一个人，他做出的选择可能会彻底改变你的生活和职业轨迹。
无扳手链接
John Ternus 将成为苹果的新 CEO。对他来说这是个好消息，实至名归，也可能让苹果在某些决策上少一些保守。近期报道中暗示的一件事是，MacBook Neo 是他的得意之作，它的成功让蒂姆·库克确信苹果交到了可靠的人手中。这似乎是积极的开端。
新单曲《Ride Lonesome》听起来很奇怪。感觉像是 Beck 故意回到专辑《Sea Change》的开篇曲《The Golden Age》，改动了几个和弦，然后就把歌交给了唱片公司。幸好他的音乐足够出色，才能像约翰·福格蒂（John Fogerty）一样随心所欲。
向新的意面酱麦克风制造商 Prego 致敬。
觉得这篇文章有意思？不妨分享给朋友！我们很快再见。
再次感谢 Setapp 的赞助。

articles.title: Is Claude Code going to cost $100/month? Probably not - it's all very confusing
articles.titleZh: Claude Code会定价100美元/月吗？可能不会——事情相当混乱
articles.summary: Anthropic悄然修改定价页面后又撤销，引发Claude Code是否将推出高价订阅模式的猜测。这种反复无常的策略反映出初创AI公司在商业化路径上的不确定性与市场试探行为。
articles.summaryEn: Anthropic's ambiguous pricing updates and reversals have fueled speculation about a potential $100/month plan for Claude Code. This erratic strategy highlights the uncertainty and market testing common among emerging AI startups navigating commercialization challenges.
articles.reason: 揭示了AI创业公司在产品商业化过程中的典型困境，为观察者提供了理解市场动态的独特窗口。

blocks.text: 22nd April 2026

Anthropic today quietly (as in silently, no announcement anywhere at all) updated their claude.com/pricing page (but not their Choosing a Claude plan page, which shows up first for me on Google) to add this tiny but significant detail (arrow is mine, and it’s already reverted):

The Internet Archive copy from yesterday shows a checkbox there. Claude Code used to be a feature of the $20/month Pro plan, but according to the new pricing page it is now exclusive to the $100/month or $200/month Max plans.
Update: don’t miss the update to this post, they’ve already changed course a few hours after this change went live.
So what the heck is going on? Unsurprisingly, Reddit and Hacker News and Twitter all caught fire.
I didn’t believe the screenshots myself when I first saw them—aside from the pricing grid I could find no announcement from Anthropic anywhere. Then Amol Avasare, Anthropic’s Head of Growth, tweeted:
For clarity, we’re running a small test on ~2% of new prosumer signups. Existing Pro and Max subscribers aren’t affected.
And that appears to be the closest we have had to official messaging from Anthropic.

I don’t buy the “~2% of new prosumer signups” thing, since everyone I’ve talked to is seeing the new pricing grid and the Internet Archive has already snapped a copy. Maybe he means that they’ll only be running this version of the pricing grid for a limited time which somehow adds up to “2%” of signups?
I’m also amused to see Claude Cowork remain available on the $20/month plan, because Claude Cowork is effectively a rebranded version of Claude Code wearing a less threatening hat!
There are a whole bunch of things that are bad about this.
If we assume this is indeed a test, and that test comes up negative and they decide not to go ahead with it, the damage has still been extensive:

A whole lot of people got scared or angry or both that a service they relied on was about to be rug-pulled. There really is a significant difference between $20/month and $100/month for most people, especially outside of higher salary countries.

The uncertainty is really bad! A tweet from an employee is not the way to make an announcement like this. I wasted a solid hour of my afternoon trying to figure out what had happened here. My trust in Anthropic’s transparency around pricing—a crucial factor in how I understand their products—has been shaken.
Strategically, should I be taking a bet on Claude Code if I know that they might 5x the minimum price of the product?

More of a personal issue, but one I care deeply about myself: I invest a great deal of effort (that’s 105 posts and counting) in teaching people how to use Claude Code. I don’t want to invest that effort in a product that most people cannot afford to use.

Last month I ran a tutorial for journalists on “Coding agents for data analysis” at the annual NICAR data journalism conference. I’m not going to be teaching that audience a course that depends on a $100/month subscription!

This also doesn’t make sense to me as a strategy for Anthropic. Claude Code defined the category of coding agents. It’s responsible for billions of dollars in annual revenue for Anthropic already. It has a stellar reputation, but I’m not convinced that reputation is strong enough for it to lose the $20/month trial and jump people directly to a $100/month subscription.

OpenAI have been investing heavily in catching up to Claude Code with their Codex products. Anthropic just handed them this marketing opportunity on a plate—here’s Codex engineering lead Thibault Sottiaux:

I don’t know what they are doing over there, but Codex will continue to be available both in the FREE and PLUS ($20) plans. We have the compute and efficient models to support it. For important changes, we will engage with the community well ahead of making them.
Transparency and trust are two principles we will not break, even if it means momentarily earning less. A reminder that you vote with your subscription for the values you want to see in this world.

I should note that I pay $200/month for Claude Max and I consider it well worth the money. I’ve had periods of free access in the past courtesy of Anthropic but I’m currently paying full price, and happy to do so.

But I care about the accessibility of the tools that I work with and teach. If Codex has a free tier while Claude Code starts at $100/month I should obviously switch to Codex, because that way I can use the same tool as the people I want to teach how to use coding agents.

Here’s what I think happened. I think Anthropic are trying to optimize revenue growth—obviously—and someone pitched making Claude Code only available for Max and higher. That’s clearly a bad idea, but “testing” culture says that it’s worth putting even bad ideas out to test just in case they surprise you.

So they started a test, without taking into account the wailing and gnashing of teeth that would result when their test was noticed—or accounting for the longer-term brand damage that would be caused.
Or maybe they did account for that, and decided it was worth the risk.

I don’t think that calculation was worthwhile. They’re going to have to make a very firm commitment along the lines of “we heard your feedback and we commit to keeping Claude Code available on our $20/month plan going forward” to regain my trust.
As it stands, Codex is looking like a much safer bet for me to invest my time in learning and building educational materials around.
Update: they’ve reversed it already

In the time I was typing this blog entry Anthropic appear to have reversed course—the claude.com/pricing page now has a checkbox back in the Pro column for Claude Code. I can’t find any official communication about it though.
Let’s see if they can come up with an explanation/apology that’s convincing enough to offset the trust bonfire from this afternoon!
Update 2: it may still affect 2% of signups?
Amol on Twitter:

was a mistake that the logged-out landing page and docs were updated for this test [embedded self-tweet]
Getting lots of questions on why the landing page / docs were updated if only 2% of new signups were affected.

This was understandably confusing for the 98% of folks not part of the experiment, and we’ve reverted both the landing page and docs changes.
So the experiment is still running, just not visible to the rest of the world?

translations: 今日，Anthropic 悄然（即完全无声无息，没有任何公开声明）更新了 claude.com/pricing 页面（但未更新“选择 Claude 计划”页面——我在 Google 上搜索时首先看到的是这个页面），新增了一个微小但意义重大的细节（箭头是我加的，目前该内容已被撤回）：
昨日互联网档案馆的截图显示，那里原本有一个勾选框。Claude Code 曾是每月 20 美元的 Pro 计划的特性之一，但根据新的定价页面，它现在仅限每月 100 美元或 200 美元的 Max 计划使用。
更新：别忘了查看这篇帖子的后续更新，就在这次变更上线几小时后，他们又改变了策略。
那么到底发生了什么？不出所料，Reddit、Hacker News 和 Twitter 上都炸开了锅。
我第一次看到这些截图时也不相信——除了定价表格，我在 Anthropic 的任何地方都找不到任何公告。后来，Anthropic 增长负责人 Amol Avasare 发推澄清：
为澄清事实，我们正在对约 2% 的新专业用户注册进行小范围测试。现有的 Pro 和 Max 订阅用户不受影响。
这似乎是目前最接近 Anthropic 官方表态的信息了。
我不相信所谓的“约 2% 的新专业用户注册”这种说法，因为我认识的人都看到了新的定价表格，而且互联网档案馆已经保存了副本。也许他的意思是，他们只会有限时间内展示这个版本的定价表格，而这“恰好”对应了 2% 的注册量？
我还觉得好笑的是，Claude Cowork 仍然保留在每月 20 美元的套餐中，因为 Claude Cowork 实际上就是 rebranded（重新品牌化）的 Claude Code，只是看起来不那么吓人罢了！
这件事有很多糟糕之处。
如果我们假设这确实是一次测试，而测试结果不理想，他们决定不推行下去，造成的损害依然巨大：
很多人感到害怕、愤怒，或者两者兼有——因为他们依赖的服务即将被“卷铺盖跑路”。对大多数人来说，尤其是非高薪国家的人，每月 20 美元和 100 美元之间的差距确实不小。
不确定性太糟糕了！用一条员工推文来宣布如此重要的变动，实在不是明智之举。我花了一整个下午的时间才搞明白发生了什么。我对 Anthropic 在定价透明度方面的信任动摇了——这是理解其产品的重要基础。
从战略角度看，如果我知道他们可能将产品的最低价格提高五倍，我是否还应该押注 Claude Code？
更偏向个人层面，但我也非常重视这一点：我投入了大量精力（目前已超过 105 篇文章）教人们如何使用 Claude Code。我可不想把精力花在大多数人根本负担不起的产品上。
上个月，我在 NICAR 数据新闻年会上为记者们举办了一场关于“用于数据分析的编码代理”的教程。我不会向这个群体教授依赖每月 100 美元订阅的课程！
从 Anthropic 的战略角度看，这也说不通。Claude Code 定义了编码代理这一品类，已为 Anthropic 带来数十亿美元的年度收入。它的声誉极佳，但我并不认为这份声誉足以支撑它放弃每月 20 美元的试用，直接让用户跳到每月 100 美元的订阅。
OpenAI 一直在 Codex 产品上大力投资，以追赶 Claude Code。Anthropic 刚刚给他们送上了一个现成的营销机会——以下是 Codex 工程负责人 Thibault Sottiaux 的发言：
我不知道他们那边在搞什么名堂，但 Codex 将继续在免费和 PLUS（20 美元）计划中提供。我们有计算资源和高效的模型来支持它。对于重大变更，我们会在实施前充分与社区沟通。透明度和信任是我们不会打破的原则，即使这意味着暂时少赚一些钱。请记住，你的订阅选择决定了你希望看到的世界价值观。
我要说明的是，我每月支付 200 美元使用 Claude Max，我认为这非常值得。过去 Anthropic 曾让我免费试用过一段时间，但目前我正在全额付费，并且很乐意这么做。
但我更关心我所使用和教学的工具的可访问性。如果 Codex 有免费层级，而 Claude Code 起步价是每月 100 美元，那么我显然会转向 Codex，因为这样我就能和我想教的人使用同样的工具来学习编码代理了。
我认为发生的情况是这样的。Anthropic 当然在尝试优化收入增长，有人提议将 Claude Code 仅对 Max 及以上用户开放。这显然是个坏主意，但“测试”文化认为，即使推出坏点子也值得一试，万一效果出乎意料呢？
于是他们开始了一场测试，却没有考虑到这个测试一旦被发现会引发怎样的轩然大波，也没有预见到由此造成的长期品牌损害。
或者他们确实考虑到了，但仍然决定承担这个风险。
我不认为这个决策是值得的。他们必须做出一个明确的承诺，比如“我们听到了你们的反馈，并承诺继续将 Claude Code 保留在我们每月 20 美元的套餐中”，才能重新赢得我的信任。
目前来看，Codex 对我来说是一个更安全的选择，我可以投入时间学习和围绕它构建教育材料。
更新：他们已经撤销了决定
在我撰写这篇博客的过程中，Anthropic 似乎已经改变了主意——claude.com/pricing 页面上的 Pro 栏又恢复了 Claude Code 的复选框。不过，我没有找到任何官方声明。
让我们看看他们能否提出一个足够有说服力的解释或道歉，来弥补今天下午损失的那堆信任！
更新 2：可能仍会影响 2% 的新用户注册？
Amol 在推特上说：

这是一个错误——退出登录后的着陆页和文档被更新用于这次测试 [嵌入的自推文]  
很多用户询问为什么着陆页/文档更新了，如果只有 2% 的新注册用户受到影响的话。

这对 98% 没有参与实验的人来说显然令人困惑，我们已经撤销了着陆页和文档的所有更改。
所以这个实验仍在运行，只是对世界其他地方不可见？

articles.title: Ben Thompson on Tim Cook’s Legacy
articles.summary: 文章探讨蒂姆·库克作为苹果运营大师的遗产，重点分析他从1998年加入苹果后如何彻底改革公司臃肿的运营体系。库克关闭了苹果自营工厂和仓库，将制造重心转移至中国，建立起高效的准时制（just-in-time）供应链，显著提升了运营效率。这一战略转型不仅为iPhone的规模化成功奠定基础，也体现了库克卓越的战略执行能力。作者认为，库克的真正遗产在于他精准把握时机、推动组织变革的能力。
articles.summaryEn: The article examines Tim Cook’s legacy as an operational genius at Apple, focusing on how he transformed the company’s inefficient operations upon joining in 1998. He shut down Apple’s own factories and warehouses, shifted manufacturing to China, and built a just-in-time supply chain that enabled unprecedented scale—especially for the iPhone. This strategic overhaul laid the foundation for Apple’s massive growth and demonstrated Cook’s impeccable timing in executing transformative changes.
articles.reason: 这是一篇深度剖析苹果运营转型的经典之作，揭示了库克如何将制造劣势转化为全球供应链优势，对理解现代科技企业的管理哲学极具启发。

blocks.text: Listen to this post:

It’s the nature of business that the eulogy for a chief executive doesn’t happen when they die, but when they retire, or, in the case of Apple CEO Tim Cook, announce that they will step up to the role of Executive Chairman on September 1. The one morbid exception is when a CEO dies on the job — or quits because they are dying — and the truth of the matter is that that is where any honest recounting of Cook’s incredibly successful tenure as Apple CEO, particularly from a financial perspective, has to begin.

The numbers, to be clear, are extraordinary. Cook became CEO of Apple on August 24, 2011, and in the intervening 15 years revenue has increased 303%, profit 354%, and the value of Apple has gone from $297 billion to $4 trillion, a staggering 1,251% increase.

The reason for Cook’s accession in 2011 became clear a mere six weeks later, when Steve Jobs passed away from cancer on October 5, 2011. Jobs’ death isn’t the reason Cook was chosen — Cook had already served as interim CEO while Jobs underwent treatment in 2009 — but I think the timing played a major role in making Cook arguably the greatest non-founder CEO of all time.
Zero to One
Peter Thiel introduced the concept of Zero To One thusly:

When we think about the future, we hope for a future of progress. That progress can take one of two forms. Horizontal or extensive progress means copying things that work — going from 1 to n. Horizontal progress is easy to imagine because we already know what it looks like. Vertical or intensive progress means doing new things — going from 0 to 1. Vertical progress is harder to imagine because it requires doing something nobody else has ever done. If you take one typewriter and build 100, you have made horizontal progress. If you have a typewriter and build a word processor, you have made vertical progress.
Steve Jobs made 0 to 1 products, as he reminded the audience in the introduction to his most famous keynote:

Every once in a while, a revolutionary product comes along that changes everything. First of all, one’s very fortunate if one gets to work on one of these in your career. Apple’s been very fortunate: it’s been able to introduce a few of these into the world.



In 1984, we introduced the Macintosh. It didn’t just change Apple, it changed the whole computer industry. In 2001, we introduced the first iPod. It didn’t just change the way we all listen to music, it changed the entire music industry.



Well, today we’re introducing three revolutionary products of this class. The first one: a widescreen iPod with touch controls. The second: a revolutionary mobile phone. And the third is a breakthrough Internet communications device. Three things…are you getting it? These are not three separate devices. This is one device, and we are calling it iPhone.

Steve Jobs would, three years later, also introduce the iPad, which makes four distinct product categories if you’re counting. Perhaps the most important 0 to 1 product Jobs created, however, was Apple itself, which raises the question: what makes Apple Apple?
The Cook Doctrine

“What Makes Apple Apple” isn’t a new question; it was the central question of Apple University, the internal training program the company launched in 2008. Apple University was hailed on the outside as a Steve Jobs creation, but while I’m sure he green lit the concept, it was clear to me as an intern on the Apple University team in 2010, that the program’s driving force was Tim Cook.
The core of the program, at least when I was there, was what became known as The Cook Doctrine:

We believe that we’re on the face of the Earth to make great products, and that’s not changing.



We’re constantly focusing on innovating.



We believe in the simple, not the complex.



We believe that we need to own and control the primary technologies behind the products we make, and participate only in markets where we can make a significant contribution.



We believe in saying no to thousands of projects so that we can really focus on the few that are truly important and meaningful to us.



We believe in deep collaboration and cross-pollination of our groups, which allow us to innovate in a way that others cannot.



And frankly, we don’t settle for anything less than excellence in every group in the company, and we have the self-honesty to admit when we’re wrong and the courage to change.



And I think, regardless of who is in what job, those values are so embedded in this company that Apple will do extremely well.

Cook explained this on Apple’s January 2009 earnings call, during Jobs’ first leave of absence, in response to a question about how Apple would fare without its founder. It’s a brilliant statement, but it is — as the last paragraph makes clear — ultimately about maintaining, nurturing, and growing what Jobs built.

That is why I started this Article by highlighting the timing of Cook’s ascent to the CEO role. The challenge for CEOs following iconic founders is that the person who took the company from 0 to 1 usually sticks around for 2, 3, 4, etc.; by the time they step down the only way forward is often down. Jobs, however, by virtue of leaving the world too soon, left Apple only a few years after its most important 0 to 1 product ever, meaning it was Cook who was in charge of growing and expanding Apple’s most revolutionary device yet.
Cook’s Triumphs

Cook, to be clear, managed this brilliantly. Under his watch the iPhone not only got better every year, but expanded its market to every carrier in basically every country, and expanded the line from one model in two colors to five models in a plethora of colors sold at the scale of hundreds of millions of units a year.

Cook was, without question, an operational genius. Moreover, this was clearly the case even before he scaled the iPhone to unimaginable scale. When Cook joined Apple in 1998 the company’s operations — centered on Apple’s own factories and warehouses — were a massive drag on the company; Cook methodically shut them down and shifted Apple’s manufacturing base to China, creating a just-in-time supply chain that year-after-year coordinated a worldwide network of suppliers to deliver Apple’s ever-expanding product line to customers’ doorsteps and a fleet of beautiful and brand-expanding stores. There was not, under Cook’s leadership, a single significant product issue or recall.

Cook also oversaw the introduction of major new products, most notably AirPods and Apple Watch; the “Wearables, Home, and Accessories” category delivered $35.4 billion in revenue last year, which would rank 128 on the Fortune 500. Still, both products are derivative of the iPhone; Cook’s signature 0 to 1 product, the Apple Vision Pro, is more of a 0.5.

Cook’s more momentous contribution to Apple’s top line was the elevation of Services. The Google search deal actually originated in 2002 with an agreement to make Google the default search service for Safari on the Mac, and was extended to the iPhone in 2007; Google’s motivation was to ensure that Apple never competed for their core business, and Cook was happy to take an ever increasing amount of pure profit.

The App Store also predated Cook; Steve Jobs said during the App Store’s introduction that “we keep 30 [percent] to pay for running the App Store”, and called it “the best deal going to distribute applications to mobile platforms”. It’s important to note that, in 2008, this was true! The App Store really was a great deal.

Three years later, in a July 28, 2011 email — less than a month before Cook officially became CEO — Phil Schiller wondered if Apple should lower its take once they were making $1 billion a year in profit from the App Store. John Gruber, writing on Daring Fireball in 2021, wondered what might have been had Cook followed Schiller’s advice:

In my imagination, a world where Apple had used Phil Schiller’s memo above as a game plan for the App Store over the last decade is a better place for everyone today: developers for sure, but also users, and, yes, Apple itself. I’ve often said that Apple’s priorities are consistent: Apple’s own needs first, users’ second, developers’ third. Apple, for obvious reasons, does not like to talk about the Apple-first part of those priorities, but Cook made explicit during his testimony during the Epic trial that when user and developer needs conflict, Apple sides with users. (Hence App Tracking Transparency, for example.)



These priorities are as they should be. I’m not complaining about their order. But putting developer needs third doesn’t mean they should be neglected or overlooked. A large base of developers who are experts on developing and designing for Apple’s proprietary platforms is an incredible asset. Making those developers happy — happy enough to keep them wanting to work and focus on Apple’s platforms — is good for Apple itself.

I want to agree with Gruber — I was criticizing Apple’s App Store policies within weeks of starting Stratechery, years before it became a major issue — but from a shareholder perspective, i.e. Cook’s ultimate bosses, it’s hard to argue with Apple’s uncompromising approach. Last year Apple Services generated 26% of Apple’s revenue and 41% of the company’s profit; more importantly, Services continues to grow year-over-year, even as iPhone growth has slowed from the go-go years.
China and AI

Another way to frame the Services question is to say that Gruber is concerned about the long-term importance of something that is somewhat ineffable — developer willingness and desire to support Apple’s platforms — which is, at least in Gruber’s mind, essential for Apple’s long-term health. Cook, in this critique, prioritized Apple’s financial results and shareholder returns over what was best for Apple in the long run.

This isn’t the only part of Apple’s business where this critique has validity. Cook’s greatest triumph was, as I noted above, completely overhauling and subsequently scaling Apple’s operations, which first and foremost meant developing a heavy dependence on China. This dependence was not inevitable: Patrick McGee explained in Apple In China, which I consider one of the all-time great books about the tech industry, how Apple made China into the manufacturing behemoth it became. McGee added in a Stratechery Interview:

Let me just refer back to something that you wrote I think a few months ago when you called the last 20, 25 years, like the golden age for companies like Apple and Silicon Valley focused on software and Chinese taking care of the hardware manufacturing. That is a perfect partnership, and if we were living in a simulation and it ended tomorrow, you’d give props for Apple to taking advantage of the situation better than anybody else.



The problem is we’re probably not living in the simulation and things go on, and I’ve got this rather disquieting conclusion where, look, Apple’s still really good probably, they’re not as good as they once were under Jony Ive, but they’re still good at industrial design and product design, but they don’t do any operations in our own country. That’s all dependent on China. You’ve called this in fact the biggest violation of the Tim Cook doctrine to own and control your destiny, but the Chinese aren’t just doing the operations anymore, they also have industrial design, product design, manufacturing design.

It really is ironic: Tim Cook built what is arguably Apple’s most important technology — its ability to build the world’s best personal computer products at astronomical scale — and did so in a way that leaves Apple more vulnerable than anyone to the deteriorating relationship between the United States and China. China was certainly good for the bottom line, but was it good for Apple’s long-run sustainability?

This same critique — of favoring a financially optimal strategy over long-term sustainability — may also one day be levied on the biggest question Cook leaves his successor: what impact will AI have on Apple? Apple has, to date, avoided spending hundreds of billions of dollars on the AI buildout, and there is one potential future where the company profits from AI by selling the devices everyone uses to access commoditized models; there is another future where AI becomes the means by which Apple’s 50 Years of Integration is finally disrupted by companies that actually invested in the technology of the future.
Cook’s Timing

If Tim Cook’s timing was fortunate in terms of when in Apple’s lifecycle he took the reins, then I would call his timing in terms of when in Apple’s lifecycle he is stepping down as being prudent, both for his legacy and for Apple’s future.

Apple is, in terms of its traditional business model, in a better place than it has ever been. The iPhone line is fantastic, and selling at a record pace; the Mac, meanwhile, is poised to massively expand its market share as Apple Silicon — another Jobs initiative, appropriately invested in and nurtured by Cook — makes the Mac the computer of choice for both the high end (thanks to Apple Silicon’s performance and unified memory architecture) and the low end (the iPhone chip-based MacBook Neo significantly expands Apple’s addressable market). Meanwhile, the Services business continues to grow. Cook is stepping down after Apple’s best-ever quarter, a milestone that very much captures his tenure, for better and for worse.

At the same time, the AI question looms — and it suggests that Something Is Rotten in the State of Cupertino. The new Siri still hasn’t launched, and when it does, it will be with Google’s technology at the core. That was, as I wrote in an Update, a momentous decision for Apple’s future:

Apple’s plans are a bit like the alcoholic who admits that they have a drinking problem, but promises to limit their intake to social occasions. Namely, how exactly does Apple plan on replacing Gemini with its own models when (1) Google has more talent, (2) Google spends far more on infrastructure, and (3) Gemini will be continually increasing from the current level, where it is far ahead of Apple’s efforts? Moreover, there is now a new factor working against Apple: if this white-labeling effort works, then the bar for “good enough” will be much higher than it is currently. Will Apple, after all of the trouble they are going through to fix Siri, actually be willing to tear out a model that works so that they can once again roll their own solution, particularly when that solution hasn’t faced the market pressure of actually working, while Gemini has?



In short, I think Apple has made a good decision here for short term reasons, but I don’t think it’s a short-term decision: I strongly suspect that Apple, whether it has admitted it to itself or not, has just committed itself to depending on 3rd-parties for AI for the long run.

As I noted above and in that Update, this decision may work out; if it doesn’t, however, the sting will be felt long after Cook is gone. To that end, I certainly hope that John Ternus, the new CEO, was heavily involved in the decision; truthfully, he should have made it.

To that end, it’s right that Cook is stepping down now. Jobs might have been responsible for taking Apple from 0 to 1, but it was Cook that took Apple from 1 to $436 billion in revenue and $118 billion in profit last year. It’s a testament to his capabilities and execution that Apple didn’t suffer any sort of post-founder hangover; only time will tell if, along the way, Cook created the conditions for a crash out, by virtue of he himself forgetting The Cook Doctrine and what makes Apple Apple.
I wrote a follow-up to this Article in this Daily Update.

translations: 听我说这个帖子：
企业界的常态是，首席执行官的悼词并非在他们去世时响起，而是在他们退休时，或如苹果CEO蒂姆·库克那样，宣布将于9月1日升任执行董事长时才响起。唯一令人不安的例外是，当一位CEO在工作岗位上离世——或因身患绝症而辞职时——事实是，任何对库克作为苹果CEO极其成功的任期（尤其是从财务角度看）的诚实回顾，都必须从这里开始。
数据可以说明一切，这些数字堪称非凡。库克于2011年8月24日接任苹果CEO，在随后的15年里，收入增长了303%，利润增长354%，苹果市值从2970亿美元飙升至4万亿美元，增幅高达1251%，令人震惊。
库克在2011年上任的原因在仅仅六周后就变得清晰起来——史蒂夫·乔布斯于2011年10月5日因癌症去世。乔布斯的离世并非库克被选中的原因——库克早在2009年就曾代理CEO职务，当时乔布斯正在接受治疗——但我认为这一时机在某种程度上起到了关键作用，使库克成为有史以来最杰出的非创始人CEO之一。
从零到一
彼得·蒂尔这样引入“从零到一”的概念：
当我们思考未来时，我们期待进步。这种进步有两种形式。横向或扩展性进步意味着复制已有的成功——从1到n。横向进步很容易想象，因为我们已经见过它的样子。纵向或密集型进步意味着做全新的事情——从0到1。纵向进步更难想象，因为它要求人们去做从未有人做过的事情。如果你有一台打字机并造出100台，那就是横向进步。如果你有一台打字机并造出一台文字处理器，那就是纵向进步。
史蒂夫·乔布斯创造了“从零到一”的产品，正如他在最著名的主题演讲开场时提醒观众的那样：

偶尔，一种革命性的产品会出现，改变一切。首先，如果你职业生涯中能参与其中一项，那真是幸运。苹果很幸运：它已经向世界推出了几款这样的产品。

1984年，我们推出了Macintosh。它不仅改变了苹果，也改变了整个计算机行业。2001年，我们推出了第一代iPod。它不仅改变了我们听音乐的方式，也彻底改变了整个音乐产业。

今天，我们将推出三款属于这一类别的革命性产品。第一项：一款带触摸控制的大屏幕iPod。第二项：一款革命性的手机。第三项：一款突破性的互联网通信装置。这三项……你明白了吗？它们不是三种独立设备。这是一台设备，我们称之为iPhone。
三年后，史蒂夫·乔布斯还推出了iPad，这使得苹果拥有了四个不同的产品类别（如果算上Mac的话则是五个）。然而，乔布斯创造的最重要的“从零到一”产品或许是苹果公司本身，这就引出一个问题：是什么让苹果成为苹果？
“是什么让苹果成为苹果”并非一个新问题；它是苹果大学（Apple University）内部培训项目的核心议题，该项目于2008年由公司启动。外界普遍认为这是史蒂夫·乔布斯的创意，虽然我确信他批准了这个构想，但作为2010年在苹果大学团队担任实习生的我清楚看到，真正推动这个项目的是蒂姆·库克。

“What Makes Apple Apple” isn’t a new question; it was the central question of Apple University, the internal training program the company launched in 2008. Apple University was hailed on the outside as a Steve Jobs creation, but while I’m sure he green lit the concept, it was clear to me as an intern on the Apple University team in 2010, that the program’s driving force was Tim Cook.
该项目的核心，至少在我任职期间是如此，后来被称为“库克原则”：

我们相信，我们存在于世的目的就是打造伟大的产品，这一点永远不会改变。  
我们始终专注于创新。  
我们相信简单胜于复杂。  
我们相信必须掌控我们制造产品背后的核心技术，只参与那些我们能做出重大贡献的市场。  
我们相信要对数千个项目说“不”，以便真正聚焦于对我们真正重要且有意义的那少数几个。  
我们相信团队间的深度协作与交叉融合，这使我们能够以他人无法企及的方式进行创新。  
坦率地说，我们对公司每个部门都要求卓越，并拥有自我反省的诚实态度，承认错误，并有勇气去改变。  
我认为，无论谁在什么岗位上，这些价值观都已深深植根于这家公司，因此苹果必将取得巨大成功。
库克在乔布斯首次请假期间的2009年1月财报电话会议上解释了这一点，回应了关于苹果如何在没有创始人的情况下继续发展的提问。这是一段精彩的陈述，但正如最后一段所明确的那样，它最终是为了维护和培育乔布斯所建立的一切。
这就是我为何在此篇文章开头强调库克接任CEO时机的重要原因。对于跟随标志性创始人之后的CEO而言，通常的情况是：将公司从零带到一的那个人往往会再待上两三年甚至更久；等到他们退位时，前方的道路往往只剩下下坡。然而，乔布斯因英年早逝，在苹果推出其历史上最重要的“从0到1”产品仅几年后就离开了世界，这意味着库克是在负责管理和扩展苹果迄今为止最具革命性的设备——iPhone。
库克的成就
需要说明的是，库克出色地完成了这一任务。在他的领导下，iPhone不仅每年都在进步，还将其市场扩展至全球几乎所有运营商所在的国家，产品线也从最初的一个型号两种颜色，发展到如今每年销售数亿台的五个型号、多种颜色的丰富选择。

毫无疑问，库克是一位运营天才。而且，在他将iPhone推向前所未有规模之前，这种才能就已显现。1998年库克加入苹果时，公司的运营体系——以自有工厂和仓库为核心——已成为沉重的负担；库克有条不紊地关闭了这些设施，并将苹果的制造业基地转移至中国，建立起一套准时制供应链，每年协调全球供应商网络，将不断扩大的苹果产品线送达客户手中，同时支撑起一支由精美门店组成的品牌扩张舰队。在库克掌舵期间，从未发生过任何重大的产品问题或召回事件。
库克还主导推出了多款重要新产品，其中尤为突出的是AirPods和Apple Watch；去年“可穿戴设备、家居与配件”类别创造了354亿美元的收入，足以跻身《财富》500强第128位。不过，这两款产品仍属于iPhone的衍生品；而库克标志性的“从0到1”之作——Apple Vision Pro，则更像是一个“从0.5到1”的过渡产品。
库克对苹果营收的最大贡献在于推动了服务业务的崛起。谷歌搜索合作协议实际上始于2002年，当时双方约定将谷歌设为Mac上Safari浏览器的默认搜索引擎，并于2007年扩展至iPhone；谷歌的动机是确保苹果永远不会与其核心业务竞争，而库克则乐于接受日益增长的高额纯利润。
App Store也早于库克时代就已存在；史蒂夫·乔布斯在介绍App Store时曾表示：“我们保留30%以支付运营成本”，并称其为“向移动平台分发应用程序的最佳交易”。值得注意的是，在2008年，这确实是一笔划算的交易！当时的App Store确实非常划算。
三年后，即2011年7月28日——就在库克正式接任CEO前不到一个月——菲尔·席勒（Phil Schiller）曾发邮件询问是否应降低抽成比例，因为当时App Store每年已为公司带来10亿美元利润。约翰·格鲁伯（John Gruber）在2021年发表于Daring Fireball的文章中提出设想：若库克采纳了席勒的建议，情况又会如何？

在我的想象中，过去十年间若苹果以菲尔·席勒那份备忘录为蓝图发展App Store，如今的世界将更加美好：开发者无疑受益，用户亦然，甚至苹果自身也会更好。我常强调苹果优先级的连贯性：首先是苹果自身需求，其次是用户需求，最后才是开发者需求。出于显而易见的原因，苹果不愿公开谈论其“苹果优先”这一部分，但库克在Epic诉讼案作证时明确表示，当用户与开发者的需求发生冲突时，苹果会选择站在用户一边（例如App Tracking Transparency政策便是如此）。

这些优先级本应如此。我并不质疑它们的排序。但将开发者需求置于第三位，并不意味着可以忽视或无视他们。一个由熟悉苹果专有平台开发与设计的专家级开发者群体构成的庞大生态，本身就是一项无可替代的资产。让这些开发者感到满意——满意到愿意持续投入并专注于苹果的平台——这对苹果自身而言也是有益的。
我想认同格鲁伯的观点——我在创办Stratechery初期就批评过苹果的App Store政策，远早于该问题成为焦点之时——但从股东视角来看，即库克的最终上级，很难反驳苹果一贯坚持的强硬立场。去年，苹果服务业务贡献了公司26%的收入和41%的利润；更重要的是，尽管iPhone增长已放缓，服务业务仍保持同比增长。
中国与人工智能
另一种看待服务业务的方式是：格鲁伯担忧的是某种难以言喻却至关重要的长期因素——开发者对支持苹果平台的意愿与渴望——在他看来，这是苹果长期健康发展的基石。在此批评框架下，库克优先考虑了苹果的财务表现与股东回报，而非长远利益。
这种批评在苹果业务的另一个领域同样具有合理性。正如我之前提到的，库克最大的胜利在于彻底改革并随后扩展了苹果的运营体系，首要任务就是建立对中国的深度依赖。这种依赖并非不可避免：帕特里克·麦基（Patrick McGee）在其著作《苹果在中国》中详细阐述了他如何推动中国成为如今庞大的制造业中心，而这本书堪称科技行业不朽的经典。他在一次 Stratechery 访谈中补充道：

我想引用你几个月前说过的一句话——你把过去二三十年称为像苹果和硅谷这类软件公司发展的黄金时代，而中国则负责硬件制造。这确实是一种完美的合作模式。如果我们生活在模拟世界中，明天就结束，我也会承认苹果比其他任何人都更善于利用这一局面。

问题是，我们很可能并不生活在模拟世界里，现实仍在持续，而我得出一个令人不安的结论：苹果或许依然表现不错，但已不如乔尼·艾夫（Jony Ive）时期那般出色。他们仍然擅长工业设计和产品设计，但所有运营都不再由美国本土掌控，而是完全依赖中国。你曾称这是对蒂姆·库克“掌控自身命运”原则的最大背离，但现在的情况更糟——中国人不仅承担运营工作，还涉足工业设计、产品设计和制造设计。
这实在讽刺：蒂姆·库克打造的技术或许是苹果最重要的能力——以惊人的规模生产世界顶级个人电脑产品——却让苹果比任何企业都更容易受到中美关系恶化的冲击。中国无疑为苹果的利润做出了贡献，但从长远来看，这对苹果是否真正有利？
同样的批评——即优先考虑财务最优策略而非长期可持续性——未来也可能被用于质疑库克留给继任者最大的问题：人工智能将对苹果产生何种影响？截至目前，苹果尚未投入数百亿美元进行 AI 基础设施建设。一种可能的未来是，苹果通过销售人们用来接入通用化模型的设备从中获利；另一种未来则是，那些真正投资于未来技术的公司最终将打破苹果长达五十年的整合优势。
库克的时代
如果说蒂姆·库克在苹果生命周期中的某个阶段接任CEO的时机是幸运的，那么他选择在当下这个阶段卸任，无论是为了他的历史地位还是苹果的未来，都可被视为审慎之举。

从传统商业模式来看，苹果如今所处的地位比以往任何时候都要好。iPhone 系列销售势头强劲，创下历史新高；与此同时，搭载 Apple Silicon 的 Mac 电脑正蓄势待发，有望大幅扩大市场份额——这同样是乔布斯时代开启的项目，如今在库克的大力投入和精心培育下，Mac 已成为高端市场（得益于 Apple Silicon 卓越的性能和统一内存架构）与入门级市场（基于 iPhone 芯片的 MacBook Neo 显著拓展了苹果可触达的用户群）的共同首选。与此同时，服务业务也持续稳步增长。库克即将卸任，而苹果刚刚创下有史以来最好的季度业绩，这一里程碑式成就既体现了他的任期成果，也反映出其中的得失成败。
然而，人工智能领域的挑战却如影随形，这似乎预示着库比蒂诺（Cupertino）的某种异样。新版 Siri 至今仍未发布，即便上线，其核心技术也将由谷歌提供。正如我在一篇更新文章中所述，这一决定对苹果的未来具有深远影响：

苹果的规划颇有几分“酗酒者承认自己有酒瘾，但承诺只在社交场合小酌”的味道。具体而言，当（1）谷歌拥有更顶尖的人才，（2）谷歌在基础设施上的投入远超苹果，（3）Gemini 模型将持续迭代升级，目前远领先于苹果的 AI 研发进展时，苹果究竟如何着手用自家模型取代 Gemini？此外，现在又多了一个不利于苹果的因素：如果这种贴牌合作方案奏效，那么“够用就好”的标准将大幅提升。试问，在历经千辛万苦终于修复 Siri 之后，苹果是否真的愿意抛弃一个已经运行良好的模型，转而重新开发自己的解决方案？更何况这套方案尚未经历真实市场的压力考验，而 Gemini 早已接受过实战检验。

简言之，我认为苹果此举是出于短期利益的合理选择，但我并不认为这是一个短期的决策：我强烈怀疑，无论苹果自身是否承认，它实际上已决定长期依赖第三方 AI 技术。
如上所述及前述更新中提及，这一决策或许最终能成功；但若失败，其后果将在库克离任后许久仍会持续发酵。为此，我衷心希望新任 CEO 约翰·特努斯（John Ternus）深度参与了该决策过程；事实上，这项决定本应由他来主导。
正因如此，库克此刻卸任可谓恰逢其时。乔布斯或许开创了苹果从无到有的辉煌，但正是库克带领公司实现了从“1”到去年高达 4360 亿美元营收和 1180 亿美元利润的飞跃。苹果并未出现创始人离世后的衰退现象，这充分证明了他的能力与执行力。至于他是否在过程中因背离“库克之道”或遗忘苹果之所以为苹果的本质，从而埋下了未来崩盘的隐患，唯有时间能给出答案。
我在本次每日更新中就此撰写了一篇后续文章。

articles.title: How to Come Up With Great Ideas
articles.summary: 文章通过一个陶艺教学实验揭示创意生成的核心逻辑：追求‘完美作品’的小组因过度规划而失败，而‘快速制作多个作品’的小组虽产出拙劣却激发了更多可能性。这说明创意往往诞生于大量试错而非精雕细琢。作者主张，在创新过程中应优先追求数量而非质量，因为多样性是突破的关键。该观点挑战了传统对‘精益求精’的推崇，强调行动力比完美主义更重要。
articles.summaryEn: An art teacher divides students into two groups: one must create one perfect pot, the other makes as many pots as possible within time limits. The first group overthinks proportions and ratios; the second produces messy, collapsing pots but generates far more ideas. The lesson is clear: creativity thrives on volume and experimentation, not perfection. Quantity breeds quality, and diversity of output increases the chance of breakthrough insights.
articles.reason: 这个简单却深刻的实验颠覆了我们对‘好创意=精心打磨’的固有认知，为任何需要创新的领域提供了可立即应用的高效思维模型。

blocks.text: There's a story about an art teacher who divides a class into two groups. The first group is given one task. Design a single, perfect pot. The second group has a different instruction entirely. Make as many pots as you can before time is up.
The first group measures, plans, and deliberates. They sketch ratios, debate proportions, and handle the clay with care. They have one shot, so they treat it like one.
The second group just makes pots. Terrible ones. pots that collapse on themselves, crack at the base, and lean sideways. They don't stop to mourn any of them, they just start the next.

When time expires, the results are revealed. The first group has a pot... technically. But it wobbles. Before the session is over, it breaks. The second group's first pot is a disaster. But their second is better. Their third, better still. By the time they've burned through a dozen attempts, they've internalized something the first group never had the chance to learn. What doesn't work. Their final pot is flawless
The second group won not because they were more talented, but because they were given the opportunity to experiment and gain experience.

When it comes to writing, it's tempting to believe you need the entire story mapped out before you begin. That you need to fully understand the premise, complete the research, and know the ending before the first sentence. That's never been true for me. When I start writing, I often don't know what it's going to be until the words appear on the page. The research doesn't precede the writing. It follows it.

I remember watching the Pixar documentary about Finding Nemo. They produced hundreds of story sketches and character drawings that never made it into the film. The director admitted that a lot of those ideas were pretty terrible. But without those sketches, they wouldn't have arrived at the final story. They learned from the volume of attempts. And we got to watch the final high quality result.

I've been following what I call the 100-times rule. For any new skill, or anything new really, I give myself permission to do it 100 times before judging the results. The goal is simple. I want to narrow the gap between idea and execution.

When I was learning to program, I'd open a blank JavaScript file and just start writing. Half the time, I'd finish a session with something unrelated to what I started. A half-baked startup idea buried in the comments, or a function that suggested an entirely new project. The work generated the ideas, not the other way around.

When I decided to write regularly, I had about a dozen ideas ready to go. I dreaded the moment I'd exhaust them. But by the time I published the twelfth post, I had several dozen more. They didn't come from some secret source of inspiration. They came from momentum. Writing regularly has trained my mind to notice things worth writing down. Conversations, observations, small frustrations, fleeting questions. The ideas were always there. I just hadn't built the habit of catching them.

This is the part most people get backwards. They wait for a great idea before they begin. But great ideas are rarely the starting point. They're the result of effort. You don't think your way to good work. You train yourself to good thinking by using repetition.

When I have an idea now, I don't wait for the right moment. I write it down immediately in my phone, in a note, sometimes directly on my blog before I fully understand it. Publishing something unpolished is uncomfortable. But an unpolished idea that's out in the world can be refined, responded to, and built upon. A perfect idea that lives only in your head cannot. In fact, I've forgotten so many great ideas!

Since the beginning of last year, I've written at least 230 articles. When I run low on new ideas, I have hundreds of old posts I can return to, deepen, and remake into something more considered. The volume created the options.
If there is any secret to coming up with great ideas it's this: Start before you're ready.

The first group in that classroom failed not because they were less capable, but because they optimized for perfection at the expense of experience. Every moment they spent planning was a moment they weren't learning what the clay actually does in your hands.
If you're waiting for the perfect idea, the right time, or enough certainty before you begin then you are the first group. You have one pot and you're protecting it.
Make more pots.

translations: 有个关于艺术老师的故事，他把一个班级分成两组。第一组的任务是设计一个完美无瑕的陶罐。第二组的指令完全不同——在时间结束前尽可能多地制作陶罐。
第一组仔细测量、认真规划、反复推敲。他们绘制比例图，争论各部分的比例关系，小心翼翼地处理黏土。因为他们只有一次机会，所以格外慎重。
第二组则只管不停地做陶罐。做出来的都是糟糕的作品：罐子自己塌了，底部开裂，歪歪斜斜。他们不为任何一个失败品哀悼，而是立刻开始下一个。
时间到了，结果揭晓。第一组确实完成了一个陶罐……技术上说是完成了。但它摇摇晃晃，还没等展示结束就碎了。第二组的第一个作品是个灾难。但第二个更好，第三个又更好。当他们尝试到第十二次时，已经领悟到第一组永远无法学到的东西——什么行不通。他们的最终作品完美无缺。
第二组之所以获胜，不是因为更有天赋，而是因为他们获得了实验和积累经验的机会。
写作时，人们常以为必须把整个故事框架搭好才能动笔，必须完全理解主题、做完调研、知道结局，才开始写第一句话。这从来不是我的情况。我写作时常常直到字词出现在屏幕上，才知道自己在写什么。研究不是先于写作，而是跟随写作而来。
我记得看过皮克斯关于《海底总动员》的纪录片。他们画出了数百个从未出现在电影里的故事草图和人设。导演承认其中很多想法都很糟糕。但没有这些草稿，他们就无法抵达最终的故事。他们是从大量尝试中学习，而我们看到了高质量的结果。
我一直遵循所谓的"百次法则"：对于任何新技能，或者任何新事物，我都允许自己在评判结果前先做一百遍。目标很简单——我想缩小想法与执行之间的差距。
我刚开始学编程时，会打开一个空的JavaScript文件就开始写。一半情况下，结束时写的已经和开头无关了——要么是藏在注释里的半成品创业点子，要么是一个暗示全新项目的函数。是工作本身产生了想法，而不是反过来。
当我决定定期写作时，手头大概有十来个现成的想法。我害怕把这些都用光的那天。但到第十二篇文章发表时，我已经有了几十个新想法。它们不是来自某个神秘的灵感源泉，而是来自持续写作带来的惯性。规律写作训练了我的大脑去注意到值得记录的东西——对话、观察、小烦恼、一闪而过的疑问。想法一直都在那里，只是我没有养成捕捉它们的习惯。
大多数人搞反了这一点。他们等到伟大的想法出现才开始行动。但伟大的想法很少是起点，而是努力的结果。你不是靠思考做出好作品，而是通过重复练习把自己训练成善于思考的人。
现在我有了想法，就不再等待所谓的时机。我会立刻用手机记下来，或者写在便签上，有时甚至直接写进博客，即便我还没完全理解它。发布一篇未经打磨的内容让我很不舒服。但一个被抛到世界上的粗糙想法可以被完善、回应和扩展；而一个只存在于你脑海中的完美想法则不能。事实上，我已经忘记了很多很棒的想法！
从去年开始，我至少写了230篇文章。当我的新点子枯竭时，我有数百篇旧文章可以回顾、深化，并重新加工成更成熟的作品。数量带来了选择的可能性。
如果说有什么秘诀能产生伟大的创意，那就是：在你准备好之前就动手去做。
教室里的第一组人之所以失败，并不是因为他们能力不足，而是因为他们为了追求完美而牺牲了体验。每一分花在计划上的时间，都意味着少了一分在手中感受黏土真实特性的机会。
如果你在等待那个“完美的想法”、恰当的时机或足够的确定性才开始，那你就是第一组人。你只有一个陶罐，还在小心翼翼地守护着它。
多做几个陶罐。

articles.title: [RSS Club] How do you preserve an RSS feed?
articles.summary: 文章讨论数字内容长期保存的挑战，引用Martin Paul Eve的观点指出博客存档面临技术过时、平台依赖和元数据丢失等问题。作者认同保存的重要性，但也认为当前技术手段尚不成熟，需付出‘必要痛苦’。文中未提出具体解决方案，但呼吁社区重视信息持久性。该文属于RSS Club系列，旨在推动开放网络标准的延续。
articles.summaryEn: This post addresses the difficulties of preserving RSS feeds beyond their original lifespan, citing Martin Paul Eve’s argument that blog archiving faces challenges like technological obsolescence and platform dependency. While agreeing preservation is crucial, the author acknowledges current methods are inadequate, calling it 'the necessary pain involved in blogging.' The piece underscores the urgency of developing sustainable digital preservation strategies for open web content.
articles.reason: 在信息快速消亡的数字时代，这篇文章提醒我们关注内容的长期存续问题，尤其适合关心Web去中心化与文化遗产保护的技术爱好者阅读。

articles.title: When Escom bought Commodore
articles.summary: 1995年4月22日，欧洲公司Escom以1400万美元收购Commodore，当时被视为Amiga平台的救星。然而收购后Escom未能扭转Commodore衰败趋势，最终导致其再次破产。文章回顾这段历史，指出Escom虽具资金实力，但缺乏对消费电子市场的深刻理解与产品创新能力。此次收购成为计算机史上一次典型的‘善意干预失败’案例。
articles.summaryEn: On April 22, 1995, Escom acquired Commodore for $14 million, offering hope for the Amiga platform’s revival. Despite initial optimism, Escom failed to reverse Commodore’s decline due to mismanagement and lack of innovation. The acquisition ultimately ended in bankruptcy, illustrating how even well-funded buyouts can falter without deep industry insight or product vision.
articles.reason: 了解Escom与Commodore的并购史，有助于看清科技企业兴衰背后的关键因素——不仅是资本，更是战略远见与市场适应力。

blocks.text: Escom acquired Commodore on April 22, 1995 for $14 million. At the time, it seemed like Commodore’s long nightmare might be over. The Amiga had a new owner. Sadly, it didn’t work out that way.
Escom: Well known in Europe, not so much in North America

Commodore had gone bankrupt just under a year earlier. Escom was the only bidder for its assets. $14 million is more money than most of us keep laying around, and it was more money in 1995 than it is now, but it was still a low risk flyer, relatively speaking. Microsoft sunk 150 million into Apple in 1997, and they didn’t even get voting shares, let alone controlling interest, for that.

Commodore’s UK subsidiary wanted to buy the whole company and had $14 million to make a minimum bid, but figured they needed $14 million to cover the first year’s operations, and they couldn’t line up the funding.

Until the announcement, Escom was mostly unknown in North America. When I received the news, my immediate reaction was to ask who is Escom? And that was the first question the American journalists covering it set out to answer. Escom was the second largest PC manufacturer in Germany and 10th largest in Europe.
Why Escom wanted Commodore

Escom had designs on being much bigger than merely #2 in Germany and #10 in Europe. That was one reason they were interested in Commodore. The Commodore brand name had recognition the Escom name lacked, and the Amiga technology arguably still could have become a viable platform in the right hands. The Amiga install base was still sizeable, with about 4.9 million units sold. And the big-box Amigas like the Amiga 4000 owned the video production market at the time, so the Amiga had a good-sized professional niche to sell into.
Escom and Commodore had a short time together

Unfortunately for everyone involved, Escom filed bankruptcy on July 15, 1996, approximately 14 months later. At the time, in 1996, ZDNet called Escom’s quick demise “astonishing.” It wasn’t that Commodore and Amiga were cursed, at least I don’t think so. Escom tried to grow too quickly. It had two once-in-a-lifetime opportunities in rapid succession: buying Commodore and buying a UK computer retailer. Unfortunately it didn’t have the cashflow to carry out both acquisitions properly.

The rights to the Amiga changed hands again soon after, landing at another troubled PC maker, Gateway 2000. The cynical joke was that after landing at Gateway, the Amiga would skip another letter of the alphabet, land at IBM, and take down IBM next. After all, the sequence of Amiga ownership went from Amiga to Commodore to Escom to Gateway.
Bits and pieces of Commodore scattered to the winds in the 1990s, especially after Escom went under.

David Farquhar is a computer security professional, entrepreneur, and author. He has written professionally about computers since 1991, so he was writing about retro computers when they were still new. He has been working in IT professionally since 1994 and has specialized in vulnerability management since 2013. He holds Security+ and CISSP certifications. Today he blogs five times a week, mostly about retro computers and retro gaming covering the time period from 1975 to 2000.

translations: 1995年4月22日，Escom以1400万美元收购了Commodore。当时看来，Commodore漫长的噩梦似乎终于要结束了。Amiga有了新主人。可惜事与愿违。
Escom在欧洲广为人知，但在北美却鲜有耳闻。
Commodore在不到一年前刚刚破产，Escom是其资产的唯一竞标者。1400万美元对大多数人来说都不是小数目，而且1995年的1400万美元比现在更值钱；但相对而言，这仍是一笔高风险投资。微软在1997年向苹果注资1.5亿美元，甚至没有获得投票权，更不用说控股权了。
Commodore的英国子公司有意收购整个公司，手头正好有1400万美元可用于最低出价，但他们估算这笔钱仅够支撑第一年的运营，且无法筹集额外资金。
直到宣布收购前，Escom在北美几乎无人知晓。当我得知消息时，第一反应就是“Escom是谁？”而这也是美国记者报道此事时首先要回答的问题。Escom是德国第二大PC制造商，欧洲第十大PC制造商。
Escom为何想要收购Commodore？
Escom志在成为超越德国第二、欧洲第十的更大企业。这是他们收购Commodore的原因之一。Commodore品牌知名度弥补了Escom品牌的不足，而Amiga技术若由合适的人接手，或许仍能发展为可行的平台。Amiga装机量仍有约490万台，当时大型机如Amiga 4000牢牢占据视频制作市场，因此Amiga拥有相当可观的专业用户群体。
Escom与Commodore的合作非常短暂。
不幸的是，Escom于1996年7月15日申请破产，仅维持了约14个月。当时ZDNet称Escom的迅速倒闭“令人震惊”。我认为Amiga和Commodore并非注定倒霉，而是Escom扩张过快所致。他们接连抓住两次千载难逢的机会：收购Commodore和英国电脑零售商，却无力同时支撑两项收购所需的现金流。
Amiga的版权很快再次易主，落入另一家陷入困境的PC厂商Gateway 2000手中。讽刺的是，坊间传言Amiga将跳过字母表中的下一个字母，直接归于IBM，并最终拖垮IBM——毕竟Amiga的东家更迭顺序是：Amiga → Commodore → Escom → Gateway。
1990年代，尤其是Escom破产后，Commodore的资产逐渐散佚各地。
David Farquhar是计算机安全专家、企业家兼作家。自1991年起专业从事计算机相关写作，因此他最早开始撰写复古电脑相关文章时，这些设备本身仍是新鲜事物。自1994年起他便投身IT行业，自2013年起专注于漏洞管理工作。他持有Security+和CISSP认证。如今他每周写博客五次，主要探讨1975至2000年间的复古电脑与复古游戏。

articles.title: [Sponsor] Rec League
articles.titleZh: Rec League：发现你热爱的世界
articles.summary: Rec League是一款新推出的推荐分享应用，用户可创建个性化收藏集（如‘罗马旅行指南’‘我的书架’），并关注值得信赖的意见领袖以获取独特见解。该应用被App Store评为‘最佳新应用’，用户评价其为‘使用后感觉更好的唯一社交媒体’。它重新定义了轻量级社交与信息发现体验。
articles.summaryEn: Rec League is a new app for curating and sharing recommendations—think personalized collections like 'bookshelf' or 'Rome travel guide'—and following trusted voices to discover unexpected insights. Recently named 'Best New App' by the App Store, users praise it as 'the only social media I feel better after using.' It offers a refreshing alternative to algorithm-driven platforms by prioritizing human curation and meaningful discovery.
articles.reason: 如果你厌倦了主流社交媒体的信息噪音，Rec League提供了一种更积极、更有价值的数字生活方式选择。

blocks.text: Share what you're into.

Rec League is an app for sharing the things you love. Catalog your recommendations into tidy collections (your guide to Rome, your open tabs, auction lots, bookshelf, things you can't put down). Follow people whose perspective you admire, connect over your shared interests, and find brilliant, unexpected intel.
"My cortisol lowers when I'm on Rec League"
— Londyn
"The only social media I feel better after using"
— Sophie
"A windowsill I can stack things up on"
— Stephanie
